coredata.dc:title: Interfacial carbon dots introduced distribution-structure modulation of Pt loading on graphene towards enhanced electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Xiao, He
Bai, Meng
Zhao, Man
Fu, Zimei
Wang, Wenxiang
Zhao, Peipei
Ma, Jiamin
Zhang, Li
Zhang, Junming
He, Yingluo
Zhang, Jian
Jia, Jianfeng
coredata.dc:description: To easily load Pt on smoothy graphene synthesized by cathodic exfoliation method and achieve adjacent plane distribution of Pt, carbon dots (CDs) are used to construct anchoring points to load highly dispersed Pt species due to strong interaction between CDs and Pt species. The composite of Pt-CDs/graphene is synthesized via a continuous process of cathodic exfoliation-hydrothermal-impregnation-reduction. Characterization results indicate the distribution configuration of Pt varies from coated structure of CDs@Pt to dispersed configuration of CDs&Pt or Pt&CDs, then to wrapping configuration of Pt@CDs with increased amount of CDs. It’s found that suitable introduction of CDs promotes the adjacent plane distribution of Pt species. The obtained best Pt-4CDs/G shows the low overpotential of 36 mV (10 mA⋅cm−2) and high mass activity of 3747.8 mA mg−1 at −40 mV towards electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), 9.2 times more active than that of Pt/C (406.2 mA mg−1). The superior HER performance of Pt-4CDs/G is attributed to its relatively adjacent plane distribution of Pt, which supports high electrochemically active surface area and more adjacent Pt sites for H* adsorption. Benefitting from that, the HER process for Pt-4CDs/G favorably follows the Tafel pathway, resulting in low hydrogen adsorption free energy and excellent HER activity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Pt
Hydrogen evolution reaction
Distribution-structure

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21571119 ), Fundamental Research Program of Shanxi Province (No. 202203021221130, No. 202203021221136, No. 20210302124473), the Natural Science Foundation of Shanxi Province (No. 202203021221130 , No. 202203021221136 , No. 20210302124473 ), “Chunhui Plan” Cooperative Scientific Research Project of Education Ministry (No. HZKY20220510), Scientific and Technological Innovation Programs of Higher Education Institution in Shanxi (2019L0466), the Graduate Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Province (2021Y480, 2022Y483), the China postdoctoral Science Foundation ( 2021M691366 ), the Graduate Education Innovation Project of Shanxi Normal University (2021XSY038) and 1331 Engineering of Shanxi Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic plot of synthesizing the Pt–CDs/graphene composites.
(a) XRD spectra and (b) amplified XRD spectra of these obtained samples.
(a) Raman spectra of different samples and (b) relationship between ID/IG and Pt loading amount.
Representative TEM micrographs of Pt/G (a), Pt-2CDs/G (b), Pt-4CDs/G (c) and Pt-6CDs/G (d). Inset in panel shows the nanoparticle core size histogram.
Representative HRTEM micrographs of CDs/G (a), Pt/G (b), Pt-2CDs/G (c), Pt-4CDs/G (d), Pt-6CDs/G(e); (f) relationship between distribution configuration and CDs amount.
CO-pulse profiles of Pt/G (a), Pt-2CDs/G (b), Pt-4CDs/G (c) and Pt-6CDs/G (d).
Pt 4f XPS spectra of as-prepared Pt-based samples.
UPS spectra of as-prepared Pt-based samples and correspond calculated work function.

(a) LSV (area activity), (b) corresponding overpotential, (c) LSV (mass activity), (d) the corresponding mass activity at −40 mV, (e) Tafel curves, (f) EIS curves and (g) the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of as-prepared samples for HER respectively, (h) LSV curves of Pt-4CDs/G before and after 2000 CV cycles in the stability test in 0.5 M H2SO4 for the HER.
(a) CV curves of as-prepared samples for H adsorption and (b) calculated Ea values based on typical Arrhenius plots.
Schematic illustration of influence of CDs amount on reaction mechanism.

head.ce:title: Interfacial carbon dots introduced distribution-structure modulation of Pt loading on graphene towards enhanced electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing and School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Solidification Processing and School of Materials Science and Engineering, Northwestern Polytechnical University, Xi’an, Shaanxi 710072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To easily load Pt on smoothy graphene synthesized by cathodic exfoliation method and achieve adjacent plane distribution of Pt, carbon dots (CDs) are used to construct anchoring points to load highly dispersed Pt species due to strong interaction between CDs and Pt species. The composite of Pt-CDs/graphene is synthesized via a continuous process of cathodic exfoliation-hydrothermal-impregnation-reduction. Characterization results indicate the distribution configuration of Pt varies from coated structure of CDs@Pt to dispersed configuration of CDs&Pt or Pt&CDs, then to wrapping configuration of Pt@CDs with increased amount of CDs. It’s found that suitable introduction of CDs promotes the adjacent plane distribution of Pt species. The obtained best Pt-4CDs/G shows the low overpotential of 36 mV (10 mA⋅cm−2) and high mass activity of 3747.8 mA mg−1 at −40 mV towards electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), 9.2 times more active than that of Pt/C (406.2 mA mg−1). The superior HER performance of Pt-4CDs/G is attributed to its relatively adjacent plane distribution of Pt, which supports high electrochemically active surface area and more adjacent Pt sites for H* adsorption. Benefitting from that, the HER process for Pt-4CDs/G favorably follows the Tafel pathway, resulting in low hydrogen adsorption free energy and excellent HER activity.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ultrathin 2D materials can be synthesized by exfoliating bulk crystals via electrochemical exfoliation method[1–8]. This electrochemical exfoliation method exhibits specific advantages of facile, scalable, low-cost and high yield. In the process of electrochemical exfoliation, ionic species could migrate and insert into the interlayer space with strong impetus of working voltage. Then these inserted species translate into gaseous species, leading to volumetric expansion forces for exfoliating bulk crystals into 2D nanosheets. Considering the difference of used electrode, these electrochemical exfoliation methods are divided into anodic exfoliation method and cathodic exfoliation method. For anodic exfoliation process, plentiful oxidative species are generated and drastic reaction carry out accompanied with anodic intercalation, contributing to formation of structure defects or other functional groups in 2D nanosheets[9]. While for cathodic exfoliation process, due to relatively slow intercalation and reduction environment, these obtained 2D samples display characteristics of little defects (pure phase), large size and high crystallinity[7]. Thus, these gained graphene prepared by cathodic exfoliation method shows superior conductivity, but smooth surface with few functional groups or defects. It means metal nanoparticles are difficultly loaded and dispersed over these smoothy graphene. This question is always on our minds.

Pt-based materials are served as the best materials for electrocatalytic hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) due to their near ideal hydrogen adsorption free energy (ΔGH)[10–12]. However, relatively high price and low reserve of Pt impedes its large-scale application. Therefore, more researchers try their best to reduce application amount of Pt and improve the atomic utilization by constructing highly dispersed single-atom or nano-size structure. To achieve our aim that reducing Pt loading-amount and easy to load Pt on smoothy graphene synthesized by cathodic exfoliation method, carbon dots (CDs) heave in sights for constructing anchoring points to load highly dispersed Pt nanoparticles (Pt NPs) on smoothy graphene. It’s found CDs could couple with graphene via π-π bonding, which not only supports abundant anchoring points for fixation and dispersion of Pt NPs, but also constructs 3D conductive network for promoting the catalytic performance[13,14]. Moreover, carbon dots also have been widely used for constructing functional materials in the electrocatalytic filed[15–17].

However, most previous works concentrate on macroscopic influence of size, conductivity, and loading amount of Pt NPs on electrocatalytic performance, but rare researches focus on controlling reaction pathway of electrocatalytic HER by distribution-structure of Pt. In HER process, if the separation between two adjacent Pt sites (dPt-Pt) is not farther than the Van der Waals radius of two adsorbed H atoms (rw), two adjacent chemical-adsorbed H atoms more likely react with each other and form into H2 molecule[18,19]. At this moment (dPt-Pt < rw), HER process follows the relatively facile and fast Tafel pathway; if the separation between two surface active sites is farther than the Van der Waals radius of two adsorbed H atoms (dPt-Pt > rw), the HER process abides by the Heyrovsky pathway. Here, H2 molecule is formed via a pathway of another adsorption and electrochemical discharge of proton on adsorbed H atom. As is concluded, for Pt-based materials, it means the separation between two adjacent Pt sites decides the reaction pathway of electrocatalytic HER. Despite of same Pt loading-amount, the distribution-structure of Pt directly affects the HER performance. If the Pt species disperse as isolated single atoms, the dPt-Pt is often greater than rw and the HER process more favorably follows the Heyrovsky pathway, but the Pt atomic utilization is very high; While the Pt species disperse as agglomerated nanoparticles, dPt-Pt of Pt spherical surface distribution exceeds the rw, and the HER process also follows the Heyrovsky pathway. However, the Pt atomic utilization is very low on this condition. Different from above two cases, the adjacent plane distribution of Pt species is more beneficial to follow the Tafel way, expected to display superior HER performance.

Based on above consideration, in this work, with a new perspective, we reveal two aspects of our research: firstly, how CDs affect distribution and electronic structure of Pt species loaded on graphene is studied; secondly, how the distribution type and electronic structure of Pt species affect reaction path of HER is investigated. This work will open a new insight for exploring the influence of distribution type on reaction path.

Graphite powder was bought from Qingdao Huatai Lubrication Sealing Technology Co., LTD. Citric acid (≥99.5 %) was obtained from Tianjin Guangfu Technology Development Co., LTD. Ethylenediamine (99 %) and ethylenediamine (99 %) were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBABF4, ≥98.0 %) and propylene carbonate (PC, ≥99.9 %) were bought from Aladdin. Ammonia solution was bought from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., LTD. Ethanol was purchased from Tianjin Beichen Founder Reagent Factory. All chemicals were not further purified.

Graphene was prepared using electrochemical cathodic exfoliation method. A certain amount of TBABF4 was added into the PC solution (15 mL) for obtaining 1 mM electrolyte solution. 40 mg of graphite powder around the platinum sheet is supported as the cathode and the other platinum sheet is served as anode. Graphene was obtained after exfoliation process for 12 h at a voltage of 30 V. Then, the obtained graphene was further calcined at 600 °C for 1 h in N2 atmosphere, and the fluffy graphene was obtained.

40 mg of above obtained graphene, 4 g of citric acid, 3.4 mL of ethylenediamine and 40 mL of NH3·H2O were added into a mixed solution of ethanol and water (1:1). Then mixed solution was treated evenly with sonication. The above solution was transferred to a Teflon liner (100 mL) for hydrothermal treatment. The initial temperature was maintained at 140 °C for 3 h, then heated to 200 °C for 3 h. After washing with ethanol, the obtained samples were dried for 6 h. Based on the added amount of carbon dots precursor, 2CDs/G, 4CDs/G, 6CDs/G were synthesized. As for 2CDs/G and 6CDs/G, the corresponding amount of ethylenediamine serve as 1.7 mL and 5.1 mL respectively.

20 mg of CDs/G sample was added into a 100 mL mixed solution of ethanol and water (3:1), which was dispersed with the help of water bath sonication. 514 µL solution of K2PtCl6 (0.1 M) was then added into the above mixture and stirred for 1 h. After that, NaBH4 is slowly added into above solution and the mixed solution is stirred for 1.5 h. Finally, the samples including Pt-2CDs/G, Pt-4CDs/G and Pt-6CDs/G are all obtained.

XRD tests were performed using an X-ray diffractometer (Ultima IV-185) with a Cu Kα1 radiation source to analyze the crystal structure. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, thermo fisher Scientific) were performed with an Al Kα X-ray source for detecting electronic structure of the as-prepared catalysts. Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, JFC-1600) were applied to study morphology of the materials. The distribution of elements was observed with an Energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS).

A series of electrochemical tests were performed on the CHI 760E workstation with a typical three-electrode system. Here, the glassy carbon electrode (GCE) was used as the working electrode. The saturated calomel electrode (SCE) was served as the reference electrode, and the graphite rod was supported as the counter electrode. When preparing the work electrode, the GCE was polished with aluminum oxide powder with a diameter of 20 ∼ 50 nm, and then was treated with ultrasonic clean in the mixed solution of ethanol and water. After that, 5 mg of catalyst and 30 μL of Nafion solution (5 wt%) were dispersed into 1 mL of mixed solution (the volume ratio of ethanol/water is 1:3), experienced ultrasound for 30 min and formed as a uniform electrocatalytic ink. Finally, 5 μL of ink droplets (0.025 mg of catalyst) were loaded on the treated GCE. These tests were carried out in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. The linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test was performed at a sweep speed of 5 mV·s−1 with the voltage range of −0.15 V-0.1 V (vs. RHE) for identifying the overpotential of HER and calculating Tafel slope. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) test is performed in the range of 105 Hz-1 Hz. For obtaining double-layer capacitance (Cdl) value, cycle voltammetry tests are performed at the sweep speed of 20 mV·s−1, 40 mV·s−1, 60 mV·s−1, 80 mV·s−1, 100 mV·s−1, 120 mV·s−1, 160 mV·s−1, 200 mV·s−1 in the non-Faraday region. The stability of obtained samples was carried out by comparing the polarization curves before and after 2000 CV cycles. All electrode potentials are converted to reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) potentials according to the Nernst equation: E (vs RHE) = E (vs SCE) + 0.241 V + 0.0591 pH.

The hybrid of Pt-CDs/graphene is synthesized via three steps (Fig. 1 ): (1) synthesis of graphene: the graphene is prepared by cathodic exfoliation method, where cheap graphite is exfoliated into graphene nanosheets due to intercalation of TBA+ in cathode with the help of electric field; (2) synthesis of CDs/graphene (CDs/G) support: the CDs/graphene hybrid is obtained via a traditional hydrothermal process, where obtained graphene is mixed with citric acid (CDs precursor) and then it experiences the coupling process; (3) synthesis of Pt-CDs/graphene (Pt-CDs/G): Pt-CDs/graphene is synthesized via a impregnation-reduction process, where Pt precursor ([PtCl4]2−) is mixed with CDs/graphene support, then it’s reduced by NaBH4 and then successfully loaded on the support.

Phase analysis of all prepared samples are carried out by XRD technique. XRD spectra (Fig. 2
a) indicates all samples display characteristic peaks of graphene (26.5° (002), 54.8° (004), PDF#08–0415)[20]. To affirm whether Pt has been loaded on the graphene, XRD spectra are amplified and showed in Fig. 2 b. For these Pt-containing materials including Pt/G, Pt-2CDs/G, Pt-4CDs/G and Pt-6CDs/G, new peaks at ∼ 39.6 corresponding to the (111) of Pt are observed, suggesting successful loading of Pt. This is further verified by ICP-OES results ( Table S1 ). For Pt/G, Pt-2CDs/G, Pt-4CDs/G and Pt-6CDs/G, their Pt contents are tested as 0.59 %, 1.00 %, 1.02 % and 1.08 % respectively. Moreover, based on the XRD spectra, average size of crystalline Pt nanoparticles can be calculated using Debye–Scherrer equation[21,22]. For Pt/G, Pt-2CDs/G, Pt-4CDs/G and Pt-6CDs/G, their average sizes ( Table S2 ) are estimated to be 5.52 nm, 5.44 nm, 4.91 nm and 5.88 nm respectively. As observed, the average size of Pt on Pt-4CDs/G is smallest among these samples, indicating much higher dispersion of Pt species.

Here, Raman spectra are applied to judge the successful loading of CDs. Observed from the Fig. 3 a, a G peak at around 1580 cm−1 and a D peak at around 1350 cm−1 are found. High ID/IG ratio reflects the substantial defects or disordered sites in the samples[2,23,24]. Previous work indicates loading of CDs over the surface of graphene will augment defects or disordered sites, meaning the increased ratio of ID/IG. Thus, high ID/IG ratio suggests high loading amount of CDs. As shown in Raman spectra, for these obtained Pt-CDs/G samples, the ID/IG ratio enhances with increased concentration of added CDs. As expected, the loading amount of CDs increases in the order: Pt-2CDs/G < Pt-4CDs/G < Pt-6CDs/G. Generally speaking, in view of strong interaction between CDs and Pt, loading amount of Pt will correspondingly increase with enhanced loading amount of CDs. But to our surprise, Pt loading amount for these Pt-CDs/G samples don’t exhibit obvious change with increased loading amount of CDs as seen in Fig. 3 b. This means the distribution configuration of Pt (or Pt&CDs) has varied with increased amount of CDs.

The dispersion and size of Pt or CDs are investigated by TEM technique. As seen from Fig. 4
a-4d, Pt nanoparticles or CDs almost obviously disperse on graphene supporter for these as-prepared catalysts, and the diameters of these nanoparticles mainly range from 1 nm to 7 nm. Their average diameters are calculated as 3.55 nm, 3.98 nm, 2.53 nm and 4.4 nm for Pt/G, Pt-2CDs/G, Pt-4CDs/G and Pt-6CDs/G respectively. It’s concluded that Pt-4CDs/G shows the smallest size among these samples, consistent with XRD results. Moreover, HRTEM images are further applied to directly reveal the distribution configuration of Pt or Pt&CDs on these as-prepared samples. As seen in Fig. 5
a, light dots are successfully loaded on graphene support. Lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is observed in CDs/G, corresponding to the (100) crystal face of graphitic carbon[12,25]. For Pt/G (Fig. 5 b), lattice spacings of 0.19 nm and 0.23 nm are found, which are assigned to (200) and (111) crystal faces of Pt. As for these Pt-CDs/G samples, both CDs and Pt nanoparticles are loaded on graphene. Noticeably, obvious different distribution configurations of Pt&CDs are also observed. For Pt-2CDs/G (Fig. 5 c), CDs is wrapped by Pt NPs and coating structure of CDs@Pt is formed. On this condition, Pt nanoparticles (Pt NPs) more easily aggerate. With increased amount of CDs, due to interaction between CDs and Pt NPs, highly dispersed types of CDs&Pt or Pt&CDs are formed over Pt-4CDs/G (Fig. 5 d). The CDs promote adjacent plane distribution of Pt species, which is expected to display superior HER performance. Different from Pt-2CDs/G and Pt-4CDs/G, with increased amount of CDs, more Pt NPs over Pt-6CDs/G are wrapped by CDs (Fig. 5 e). The structure of Pt@CDs is correspondingly formed. Just as Fig. 5 f displays, amount of CDs can be used to control the distribution configuration of Pt. If the loading amount of CDs is low, the coating structure of CDs@Pt is more easily formed as Pt-2CDs/G shows, while the loading amount of CDs is high, the coating structure of Pt@CDs is more favorably formed as Pt-6CDs/G. When the loading amount of CDs is suitable, highly dispersed structures of CDs&Pt or Pt&CDs are more generated over Pt-4CDs/G.

To investigate the influence of CDs on dispersion of Pt NPs, CO-pulse test is applied[26,27]. The CO-pulse results (Fig. 6
a–d) indicate that tested Pt dispersions for Pt/G, Pt-2CDs/G, Pt-4CDs/G and Pt-6CDs/G are 3.2 %, 11.5 %, 13.2 % and 9.8 % respectively. Clearly, dispersion of Pt in Pt-4CDs/G is higher than that in other catalysts, further verifying HRTEM and TEM results. These highly dispersed Pt NPs are beneficial for sufficient exposure of active sites and are expected to exhibit outstanding HER catalytic activity. Probe molecule-TPD is always used to identify active-site kind and verify the adsorption strength, which could reflect the electronic structure of active sites[27,28]. Thus, to further confirm electronic structure change of Pt caused by CDs, CO temperature-programmed desorption (CO-TPD) is employed ( Fig. S1 ). For pure graphene (G), it doesn’t show CO-desorption peak. With the introduction of CDs. Obtained CDs/G displays a broad CO-desorption peak from 173 °C to 319 °C. While for Pt/G, it shows a sharp peak at about 167 °C. It’s concluded that CO-desorption peak of Pt NPs is much sharper, while that of CDs is much broader. For these Pt-CDs/G samples, their CO-desorption peak resulting from Pt NPs all shift to higher temperature, especially for Pt-4CDs/G. This indicates that introduction of CDs alters the electronic structure of Pt NPs and adjusts their adsorption performance.

XPS technique is an efficient method to analyze chemical state information of elements[29]. Fig. S2 shows both C and O elements exist in these Pt-based samples. Except these two elements, Pt element also can be seen from Pt4f spectra (Fig. 7 ), the fitting results show Pt0 and Pt2+ both are present in these four Pt-base samples. Two peaks at about 71.7 eV and 75.0 eV are assigned to Pt0, while other two peaks at 73.4 eV and 76.4 eV are indexed to Pt2+. It’s found the amount of Pt2+ increases with enhanced amount of CDs, may because these CDs with abundant oxygen-containing species easily make Pt NPs oxidate. With increase of CDs, distribution configuration of Pt&CDs changes and more Pt NPs are wrapped by CDs. Thus, Pt2+ content calculated from XPS and ICP-OES for these Pt-based electrocatalysts increase in the order: Pt-2CDs/G (0.421 %) < Pt-4CDs/G (0.485 %) < Pt-6CDs/G (0.539 %).

Work function could reflect dynamic rate of the electron transfer on the surface of the catalysts[30]. If the electrons of the catalysts are more easily delivered to the reactant, the corresponding reaction is thus activated thermodynamically. Ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS) is always used to determine the values of work function of obtained materials[31]. As seen from Fig. 8 , work functions of G, Pt/G, Pt-2CDs/G, Pt-4CDs/G, Pt-6CDs/G are calculated to be 4.96 eV, 4.46 eV, 4.59 eV, 4.64 eV and 4.76 eV respectively. This clearly illustrates the work functions increase with enhanced amount of CDs. This means introduction of CDs makes the Pt-d state shift (compared with Fermi level) and the work function change originated from the interfacial Pt-CDs interaction, which will finally optimize adsorption-activation of H+ and low reaction energy barrier of whole reaction towards HER.

The electrocatalytic performance of all as-prepared samples toward HER was investigated using a standard three-electrode system (without iR correction). As seen from the linear sweep voltammograms (LSV) curves (Fig. 9
a), Pt-4CDs/G exhibits excellent HER activity among these obtained samples. Its overpotential at the current density of 10 mA⋅cm−2 is 36 mV (Fig. 9 b), comparable with that of commercial Pt/C (28 mV), and much lower than Pt/G (95 mV), Pt-2CDs/G (52 mV), Pt-6CDs/G (49 mV), CDs/G (700 mV) and graphene (649 mV). To rationally compare HER activity of Pt-4CDs/G with Pt/C, their mass activities (normalized to the current/Pt loading mass (A/mg)) was studied (Fig. 9 c). It can be seen that Pt-4CDs/G displays much higher HER catalytic activity than commercial Pt/C catalysts. The mass activities (Fig. 9 d) for Pt-4CDs/G is 3747.8 mA mg−1 at −40 mV, 9.2 times more active than that of Pt/C (406.2 mA mg−1). This obtained Pt-4CDs/G shows competitive electrocatalytic activity towards acidic HER in comparison with these platinum-based catalysts (Table S3) [13,32–38]. HER activity in alkaline solution over these obtained catalysts are also tested. Among these obtained samples (Fig. S3), Pt-4CDs/G shows the lowest overpotential of 48 mV at the current density of 10 mA⋅cm−2. The exceptional HER activity of Pt-4CDs/G can be attributed to the high dispersity and distribution configuration of ultrafine Pt NPs over Pt-4CDs/G according to previous XRD, ICP-OES, TEM and HRTEM results.

Tafel slope is applied to uncover the rate-limiting step in HER process for these sample[39]. As observed from Fig. 9 e, Tafel slope over Pt/G is 138 mV dec−1, suggesting that adsorption of H+ evolved into H* (Volmer step) is the rate-limiting step over Pt/G. With incorporation of CDs to Pt/G, the Tafel slope alters greatly. Tafel slopes over Pt-2CDs/G and Pt-6CDs/G are 48 mV dec−1 and 49 mV dec−1 respectively, meaning conversion of their rate-limiting step into desorption of H* (Heyrovsky step), while for Pt-4CDs/G, its Tafel slope is calculated as 28 mV dec−1, suggesting the conversion of rate-limiting step into Tafel step. The process of HER via Tafel mechanism indicates two adjacent Pt sites (dPt-Pt) becomes much smaller than the van der Waals radius of two adsorbed H atoms (rH-H). It suggests more electroactive Pt sites in Pt-4CDs/G than in other Pt-based sample, due to excellent high dispersion and superior distribution of Pt sites.

Consistent with the Tafel results, EIS results further verify that conductivity of Pt/G is greatly improved with the incorporation of CDs (Fig. 9 f), especially for Pt-4CDs/G, it exhibits best conductivity. Further analysis of the impedance data was carried out by performing a fitting against the Randles circuit to obtain the charge-transfer resistance values. The Rct value is 25.4 Ω for Pt-4CDs/G, much lower than that of Pt-2CDs/G (49.2 Ω), Pt-6CDs/G (43.8 Ω), Pt/G (153 Ω). Furthermore, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) of obtained samples is determined with aid of their electrochemical double-layer capacitances (Cdl)[40] (Fig. 9 g), which is inferred from the CV results ( Fig. S4 ). ECSAs of these obtained samples are positively with the OER activity. Pt-4CDs/G shows the ECSAs of 26.3 cm2 ( Table S4 ), much high than Pt-2CDs/G (20.1 cm2), Pt-6CDs/G (21.3 cm2), Pt/G (11.8 cm2). This further explains why this Pt-4CDs/G shows best HER activity. In addition, the tested LSV curve over Pt-4CDs/G remains almost unchanged before and after 2000 CV scanning (Fig. 9 h), indicating strong electrocatalytic HER durability of the Pt-4CDs/G.

Hydrogen adsorption/desorption amount obtained from CV curves in Faraday-free range can be used to verify the ECSA and reaction mechanism[41]. From Fig. 10
a, it’s seen the Pt-4CDs/G displays the most H* adsorption/desorption amount among these samples, meaning space between adjacent Pt active sites in Pt-4CDs/G turns smaller than that in other samples. Meanwhile, two adsorbed H atoms favorably convert into H2 via Tafel step, agreeing with Tafel results. This further proves that inductive effect of CDs structure results in the enhanced intrinsic activity of Pt NPs towards HER, which has been revealed by previous mass activity results. Except that, energy barrier of the overall HER process can be evaluated by ΔG* values derived from Arrhenius plots ( Fig. S5-S6 )[42–44]. It’s seen that calculated ΔG* value (Fig. 10 b) for Pt-4CDs/G is only 24.7 kJ mol−1, much lower than that of Pt-2CDs/G (40.3 kJ mol−1), Pt-6CDs/G (25.1 kJ mol−1), Pt/G (50.7 kJ mol−1). This means highly dispersion type of Pt&CDs decreases the ΔG* and enhances the intrinsic activity for HER.
Here, the influence of CDs amount on reaction mechanism is discussed in detail.

After loading on graphene, Pt species mainly exist as three forms including agglomerated nanoparticle, isolated single atom and adjacent plane polyatom (Fig. 11
a). If Pt species disperse as isolated single atoms, the H adsorption amount is enough, but the dPt-Pt is much greater than rw. While the Pt species disperse as agglomerated NPs, their spherical surface distribution decreases the dPt-Pt, but the H adsorption amount decreases. Thus, above two Pt distributions make the HER process follow the Heyrovsky pathway. To increase the H adsorption amount and decrease the dPt-Pt, the adjacent plane distribution is proposed. It’s expected that HER process follows the Tafel way on this condition and it displays superior HER performance.

In this work, the distribution of Pt on graphene supporter can be adjusted by introduction of CDs due to strong interaction between Pt and CDs (Fig. 11 b). For Pt/G, only Pt NPs are loaded on graphene supporter. Despite of ultralow loading, Pt NPs still easily aggregate into bigger size. Due to that, the adsorption amount of H* is very low, resulting in RDS of H*adsorption (Volmer step). With introduction of CDs into Pt/C, more Pt NPs are loaded on graphene because of strong interaction between them. Thus, the loading amount of Pt increases. For Pt-2CDs/G, it’s found CDs are wrapped with Pt NPs and the structure of CDs@Pt is correspondingly formed. Electronic structure of Pt species is adjusted and their H* adsorption performance is altered. Thus, the RDS converts from Volmer step into Heyrovsky step. With further increase in amount of CDs, loading amount of Pt in Pt-4CDs/G almost remains the original state compared with Pt-2CDs/G. Different from wrapping configuration of CDs@Pt in Pt-2CDs/G, highly dispersed configuration of CDs&Pt or Pt&CDs in Pt-4CDs/G are generated. Benefiting from small size, high dispersion and superior configuration, adsorption amount of H* achieve the largest amount among these Pt-based samples. This leads to much smaller dPt-Pt than the rw, suggests the increase of CDs promotes the adjacent plane distribution of Pt species. This results in conversion of RDS into favorite Tafel step. As for Pt-6CDs/G, because of too much loading amount of CDs, more Pt NPs are wrapped by CDs and enfolded configuration of Pt@CDs is formed. Compared with Pt-4CDs/G, Pt-6CDs/G shows low adsorption amount of H*and the Heyrovsky step turns into RDS. Thus, benefitting from the superior distribution of Pt, Pt-4CDs/G shows better activity and higher stability than other samples.

In summary, three aspects of work have been accomplished. For the first thing, the composite of Pt-CDs/graphene is prepared via three steps of cathodic exfoliation, hydrothermal and impregnation-reduction. Among these as-prepared Pt-CDs/G samples, the best Pt-4CDs/G shows the highest mass activity of 3747.8 mA mg−1 at −40 mV, 9.2 times more active than that of Pt/C (406.2 mA mg−1) and the lowest overpotential of 36 mV (10 mA⋅cm−2). For the second thing, characterization results such as XRD, XPS, ICP-OES, Raman, HRTEM et al. suggest introduction of CDs enhances the loading amount of Pt on graphene. But with increased loading amount of CDs, Pt loading amount for these Pt-CDs/G samples don’t show obvious change due to distribution configuration variation of Pt. For Pt-2CDs/G, coated structure of CDs@Pt is formed. With the increased amount of CDs, highly dispersed configuration of CDs&Pt or Pt&CDs are generated in Pt-4CDs/G because of strong interaction between CDs and Pt. As for Pt-6CDs/G, too much loading amount of CDs leads to package of more Pt NPs and wrapping configuration of Pt@CDs is formed. HRTEM and CO-pulse results reveal that Pt-4CDs/G shows the highest dispersion of Pt. Pt XPS and UPS test results indicate the introduction of CDs modulates the electronic structure of Pt, which optimizes adsorption-activation performance of Pt sites towards HER. For the final thing, profiting from the superior distribution of Pt in Pt-4CDs/G, the Pt-4CDs/G displays the high ECSA and supports more adjacent Pt sites for H* adsorption. This makes the HER process follow the Tafel pathway and results in low ΔG*. Thus, it’s concluded that suitable introduction of CDs promotes the adjacent plane distribution of Pt species, contributing to excellent HER behavior.

He Xiao: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Meng Bai: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Man Zhao: Supervision, Resources, Validation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Zimei Fu: Validation, Software, Methodology. Wenxiang Wang: Validation, Software, Methodology. Peipei Zhao: Validation, Software, Methodology. Jiamin Ma: Validation, Software, Methodology. Li Zhang: Supervision, Validation. Junming Zhang: Supervision, Validation. Yingluo He: Supervision, Validation. Jian Zhang: Supervision, Resources, Validation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Jianfeng Jia: Conceptualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
